attributes.stem:
A four-year-old boy presents to the Emergency Department after an unwitnessed incident. They were looking at the construction site of their new home. His parents heard him cry and rushed to find him on the floor. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. His immunisations are up to date.

On examination, he appears distressed but alert. His pulse rate is irregular at 120/min, blood pressure 100/65 mmHg, respiratory rate 24/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. There is a small circular ulcer on his right palm and another on his right foot. There are no other blisters or ulcers elsewhere. The affected limb is tender and slightly swollen but has normal pulses, sensation, and capillary refill.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This case presents classic features of likely an electrical burn injury. The presence of both entry and exit wounds (on the palm and foot) is pathognomonic of an electrical burn, as the current passes through the body seeking the path of least resistance to ground. At this young age, he is at high risk of accidental injuries in an unsafe environment such as a construction site.

High-voltage electrical burn is the most likely diagnosis given the mechanism of injury and clinical findings. Even though the surface burns appear small, there may be extensive deep tissue damage along the current path as the fluid content in blood vessels act as a conductor of electricity. The swelling suggests possible muscle damage, which can lead to compartment syndrome.

Spider bite typically presents with a single site of injury with surrounding erythema and local tissue reaction. It would not explain the presence of both entry and exit wounds or the cardiac arrhythmia.

Chemical burn would typically present with a different pattern of injury, often with spreading areas of damage and chemical residue.

Cellulitis presents with spreading erythema, warmth, and tenderness of the affected area. It would not explain the presence of discrete circular burns or cardiac arrhythmia which such acuity.

Toxic epidermal necrolysis is a severe mucocutaneous reaction typically triggered by medications, characterized by widespread epidermal detachment and mucosal involvement. It would not present with focal circular burns or cardiac arrhythmia.

Management of electrical burns includes cardiac monitoring (due to risk of arrhythmias), careful assessment for compartment syndrome, and measurement of creatine kinase to assess for rhabdomyolysis with aggressive fluid management. These patients often require admission for observation and may need surgical intervention if compartment syndrome develops.
learningPoints: Electrical burns commonly present with small entry and exit wounds that belie the extent of deep tissue damage
references: Alfred Health & VicBurns. Severe electrical burns. Victorian Adult Burns Service. 2024. Available at: https://www.vicburns.org.au/severe-burns/secondary-survey/severe-electrical-burns/#:~:text=Electr...'s%20Major%20Trauma%20criteria

Culnan DM, Farner K, Bitz GH, Capek KD, Tu Y, Jimenez C, Lineaweaver WC. Volume Resuscitation in Patients With High-Voltage Electrical Injuries. Ann Plast Surg. 2018. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5860824/
